1:
"And the LORD spake unto Moses, saying," The witnesses note the laws of the public sacrifices now renewed, the people being about to enter the land. The witnesses note that Gill notes that, the people being numbered and the land to be divided, the Lord thought proper to renew, or put them in mind of, the laws concerning sacrifices, the more needful as it was now thirty-eight years since they were first given. The witnesses note that the offerings were to be observed when they came into Canaan. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the a new word — the LORD speaking again to Moses; the the sacrifices recalled — the laws of public worship renewed; the thirty-eight years on — a new generation needing the statutes; the before the land — worship set in order before the conquest.

The witnesses agree the renewing of these laws fitted the moment: a new generation stood at the edge of the land, most of whom had not heard these statutes given, and God recalls the whole order of public worship before they enter their inheritance; the timing shows his care that the people, once settled, should serve him aright, the worship of God set in order before the conquest, that their life in the land begin with sacrifice.

So the witnesses make the verse the laws renewed: The witnesses note the a new word: the LORD speaking again to Moses. The witnesses note the the sacrifices recalled: the laws of public worship renewed. The witnesses note the thirty-eight years on: a new generation needing the statutes. The witnesses note the before the land: worship set in order before the conquest. The expositors observe the a new word: the LORD speaking again to Moses. The expositors observe the the sacrifices recalled: the laws of public worship renewed. The expositors observe the thirty-eight years on: a new generation needing the statutes. The expositors observe the before the land: worship set in order before the conquest. The believer is taught to mark the a new word, the the sacrifices recalled, the thirty-eight years on, the before the land, and to look to the one continual sacrifice. The verse thus records that God renewed the laws of public sacrifice — the worship of God set in order before the new generation entered the land, that their life there begin with sacrifice.
2:
"Command the children of Israel, and say unto them, My offering, and my bread for my sacrifices made by fire, for a sweet savour unto me, shall ye observe to offer unto me in their due season." The witnesses note the public sacrifices — daily, weekly, monthly, and yearly — called God's "bread" and "sweet savour," to be offered in due season. The witnesses note that Gill notes these concerned the whole congregation, not private persons, and might be seen not merely as commands but as tokens of God's favour, that despite all their rebellions he would still accept their offerings. The witnesses note that they were to be offered in their due season. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the my offering, my bread — the sacrifices God is pleased to receive; the a sweet savour — his gracious acceptance, not his need; the in their due season — the order and constancy of worship; the the one offering — christ a sweetsmelling savour to God (Eph. 5:2).

The witnesses agree the calling of the sacrifices God's "bread" and "sweet savour" speaks of his gracious acceptance, not of any need in him: he is pleased to receive his people's offerings as a host receives food and as a man delights in a sweet smell, the whole pointing to the one offering of Christ, who gave himself "a sacrifice to God for a sweetsmelling savour" (Eph. 5:2); and the charge to keep them "in due season" presses the order and constancy that befit the worship of God.

So the witnesses make the verse God's bread and sweet savour: The witnesses note the my offering, my bread: the sacrifices God is pleased to receive. The witnesses note the a sweet savour: his gracious acceptance, not his need. The witnesses note the in their due season: the order and constancy of worship. The witnesses note the the one offering: Christ a sweetsmelling savour to God (Eph. 5:2). The expositors observe the my offering, my bread: the sacrifices God is pleased to receive. The expositors observe the a sweet savour: his gracious acceptance, not his need. The expositors observe the in their due season: the order and constancy of worship. The expositors observe the the one offering: Christ a sweetsmelling savour to God (Eph. 5:2). The believer is taught to mark the my offering, my bread, the a sweet savour, the in their due season, the the one offering, and to look to the one continual sacrifice. The verse thus records that the sacrifices are God's bread and sweet savour, to be offered in season — his gracious acceptance pointing to the one offering of Christ, a sweetsmelling savour to God (Eph. 5:2).
3:
"And thou shalt say unto them, This is the offering made by fire which ye shall offer unto the LORD; two lambs of the first year without spot day by day, for a continual burnt offering." The witnesses note the continual burnt offering — two unblemished lambs of the first year, day by day. The witnesses note that Gill notes the Lord proceeds to order the sacrifices particularly and distinctly, this being, as Jarchi says, an admonition to the sanhedrim to see them offered. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set the two lambs without spot, day by day, for a continual burnt offering. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the two lambs — one morning, one evening; the of the first year, without spot — the unblemished victim; the day by day — offered without fail continually; the the Lamb — figuring Christ's ever-availing offering (Heb. 10:14).

The witnesses agree the daily sacrifice stands at the head of all as the foundation of Israel's worship: two spotless lambs, one each morning and evening, offered without fail every day — the continual burnt offering that figured the unceasing acceptance of God's people through sacrifice, and pointed to Christ, the Lamb without blemish, whose one offering is of perpetual and ever-present efficacy (Heb. 10:14).

So the witnesses make the verse the continual offering: The witnesses note the two lambs: one morning, one evening. The witnesses note the of the first year, without spot: the unblemished victim. The witnesses note the day by day: offered without fail continually. The witnesses note the the Lamb: figuring Christ's ever-availing offering (Heb. 10:14). The expositors observe the two lambs: one morning, one evening. The expositors observe the of the first year, without spot: the unblemished victim. The expositors observe the day by day: offered without fail continually. The expositors observe the the Lamb: figuring Christ's ever-availing offering (Heb. 10:14). The believer is taught to mark the two lambs, the of the first year, without spot, the day by day, the the Lamb, and to look to the one continual sacrifice. The verse thus records that two spotless lambs were offered day by day as a continual burnt offering — the foundation of Israel's worship, figuring Christ the Lamb without blemish, his offering of perpetual efficacy (Heb. 10:14).
4:
"The one lamb shalt thou offer in the morning, and the other lamb shalt thou offer at even;" The witnesses note the two lambs of the daily sacrifice — one in the morning, one at evening. The witnesses note that Gill notes the morning lamb was to make atonement, as the Targum says, for the sins of the night. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set the one in the morning and the other at even. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the one in the morning — the day opened with sacrifice; the the other at even — the day closed with the lamb; the atonement for the night — and for the day's sins; the the day bounded in sacrifice — life kept under the covering of blood.

The witnesses agree the morning and evening lamb framed the whole day in sacrifice: the day opened and closed with a lamb upon the altar, that Israel might begin and end in the favour of God, atonement made for the sins of the night and of the day; the doubled offering teaches the believer to bound his days with the remembrance of the one sacrifice, looking to the Lamb at morning and at evening, the whole of life kept under the covering of blood.

So the witnesses make the verse morning and evening: The witnesses note the the one in the morning: the day opened with sacrifice. The witnesses note the the other at even: the day closed with the lamb. The witnesses note the atonement for the night: and for the day's sins. The witnesses note the the day bounded in sacrifice: life kept under the covering of blood. The expositors observe the the one in the morning: the day opened with sacrifice. The expositors observe the the other at even: the day closed with the lamb. The expositors observe the atonement for the night: and for the day's sins. The expositors observe the the day bounded in sacrifice: life kept under the covering of blood. The believer is taught to mark the the one in the morning, the the other at even, the atonement for the night, the the day bounded in sacrifice, and to look to the one continual sacrifice. The verse thus records that one lamb was offered morning and one evening — the day bounded in sacrifice, Israel beginning and ending in God's favour, life kept under the covering of the Lamb.
5:
"And a tenth part of an ephah of flour for a meat offering, mingled with the fourth part of an hin of beaten oil." The witnesses note the meat offering of fine flour and beaten oil that accompanied each lamb. The witnesses note that Gill notes the meat offering always went along with the burnt offering. The witnesses note that Ellicott refers the "beaten oil" to the pure oil for the light and the offerings (Exod. 27:20). The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the a tenth of flour — the fine flour of the meat offering; the beaten oil — the pure oil mingled with it (Exod. 27:20); the with the burnt offering — the field's fruit joined to the lamb; the the oil the Spirit — grace breathing through the daily gift.

The witnesses agree the meat offering joined to the lamb completed the daily gift: the fine flour and beaten oil, the fruit of the field offered with the victim, figured the dedication of the worshipper's substance and life to God along with the sacrifice; the oil throughout, the emblem of the Spirit, breathed of the grace by which alone such service is wrought and accepted, the daily bread of the altar mingled with the oil of gladness.

So the witnesses make the verse the meat offering: The witnesses note the a tenth of flour: the fine flour of the meat offering. The witnesses note the beaten oil: the pure oil mingled with it (Exod. 27:20). The witnesses note the with the burnt offering: the field's fruit joined to the lamb. The witnesses note the the oil the Spirit: grace breathing through the daily gift. The expositors observe the a tenth of flour: the fine flour of the meat offering. The expositors observe the beaten oil: the pure oil mingled with it (Exod. 27:20). The expositors observe the with the burnt offering: the field's fruit joined to the lamb. The expositors observe the the oil the Spirit: grace breathing through the daily gift. The believer is taught to mark the a tenth of flour, the beaten oil, the with the burnt offering, the the oil the Spirit, and to look to the one continual sacrifice. The verse thus records that fine flour and beaten oil accompanied each lamb — the worshipper's substance offered with the sacrifice, the oil of the Spirit breathing through the daily gift.
6:
"It is a continual burnt offering, which was ordained in mount Sinai for a sweet savour, a sacrifice made by fire unto the LORD." The witnesses note the daily offering as the continual one ordained at Sinai, a sweet savour to the LORD. The witnesses note that Gill notes the meat offering was burnt as well as the lambs, at least part of it. The witnesses note that Ellicott renders "ordained" as "made in Mount Sinai," noting Ibn Ezra's inference that Israel ceased to offer burnt sacrifices through the years of wandering. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the a continual burnt offering — the daily, unceasing sacrifice; the ordained in mount Sinai — given with the law itself; the a sweet savour — accepted and pleasing to the LORD; the never to cease — the heart of Israel's abiding worship.

The witnesses agree the daily offering carried the authority of Sinai and the sweetness of acceptance: it was no new thing but the continual offering ordained when the law was given, now to be resumed and kept perpetually in the land; and whether or not the offering had lapsed during the wandering, as some inferred, the command renews it as a "sweet savour" — the steady, daily sacrifice that should never cease in Israel, the heart of its abiding worship.

So the witnesses make the verse ordained at Sinai: The witnesses note the a continual burnt offering: the daily, unceasing sacrifice. The witnesses note the ordained in mount Sinai: given with the law itself. The witnesses note the a sweet savour: accepted and pleasing to the LORD. The witnesses note the never to cease: the heart of Israel's abiding worship. The expositors observe the a continual burnt offering: the daily, unceasing sacrifice. The expositors observe the ordained in mount Sinai: given with the law itself. The expositors observe the a sweet savour: accepted and pleasing to the LORD. The expositors observe the never to cease: the heart of Israel's abiding worship. The believer is taught to mark the a continual burnt offering, the ordained in mount Sinai, the a sweet savour, the never to cease, and to look to the one continual sacrifice. The verse thus records that the daily offering was the continual one ordained at Sinai, a sweet savour — the steady sacrifice never to cease, the heart of Israel's abiding worship.
7:
"And the drink offering thereof shall be the fourth part of an hin for the one lamb: in the holy place shalt thou cause the strong wine to be poured unto the LORD for a drink offering." The witnesses note the drink offering of wine poured out to the LORD with each lamb. The witnesses note that Gill notes the drink offering of wine, strong wine, the fourth part of a hin, went with the meat offering for the morning lamb. The witnesses note that Clarke notes the word for "strong wine," and refers to the place where it is discussed (Lev. 10:9). The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the a fourth of a hin — the measure of wine for each lamb; the strong wine — poured out in the holy place; the with the lamb — completing the daily sacrifice; the poured out — the self-poured life, and the blood of Christ (Phil. 2:17).

The witnesses agree the drink offering completed the daily gift with a libation poured out before God: the wine, emblem of joy and of the blood, was poured out in the holy place along with the lamb and the flour, that the whole sacrifice be entire; the pouring-out of the wine figures the self-poured life of devotion, and supremely the blood of Christ poured out, the apostle taking up the very image of being "poured out" as a drink offering upon the service of faith (Phil. 2:17).

So the witnesses make the verse the drink offering: The witnesses note the a fourth of a hin: the measure of wine for each lamb. The witnesses note the strong wine: poured out in the holy place. The witnesses note the with the lamb: completing the daily sacrifice. The witnesses note the poured out: the self-poured life, and the blood of Christ (Phil. 2:17). The expositors observe the a fourth of a hin: the measure of wine for each lamb. The expositors observe the strong wine: poured out in the holy place. The expositors observe the with the lamb: completing the daily sacrifice. The expositors observe the poured out: the self-poured life, and the blood of Christ (Phil. 2:17). The believer is taught to mark the a fourth of a hin, the strong wine, the with the lamb, the poured out, and to look to the one continual sacrifice. The verse thus records that wine was poured out with each lamb as a drink offering — the libation completing the daily gift, figuring the self-poured life of devotion and the blood of Christ poured out (Phil. 2:17).
8:
"And the other lamb shalt thou offer at even: as the meat offering of the morning, and as the drink offering thereof, thou shalt offer it, a sacrifice made by fire, of a sweet savour unto the LORD." The witnesses note the evening lamb offered just as the morning, with its meat and drink offerings, a sweet savour. The witnesses note that Gill notes the other lamb was offered at even, as before directed. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set the evening lamb with the same meat and drink offerings as the morning, a sweet savour. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the other at even — the evening sacrifice; the as the morning — the same flour, oil, and wine; the a sweet savour — as acceptable as the morning offering; the constant worship — the same care at the day's end as its dawn.

The witnesses agree the evening sacrifice answered the morning exactly, closing the day as it had opened: the same lamb, the same flour and oil, the same poured wine, a sweet savour to the LORD — the worship of God not varying with the time but constant, morning and evening alike; the unchanging form teaches that God is to be served with the same care at the day's end as its beginning, the evening offering as acceptable as the morning, and the believer to close his day as he began it, looking to the Lamb.

So the witnesses make the verse the evening lamb: The witnesses note the the other at even: the evening sacrifice. The witnesses note the as the morning: the same flour, oil, and wine. The witnesses note the a sweet savour: as acceptable as the morning offering. The witnesses note the constant worship: the same care at the day's end as its dawn. The expositors observe the the other at even: the evening sacrifice. The expositors observe the as the morning: the same flour, oil, and wine. The expositors observe the a sweet savour: as acceptable as the morning offering. The expositors observe the constant worship: the same care at the day's end as its dawn. The believer is taught to mark the the other at even, the as the morning, the a sweet savour, the constant worship, and to look to the one continual sacrifice. The verse thus records that the evening lamb was offered as the morning, a sweet savour — the worship of God constant, the day closed as it opened, the believer ending his day looking to the Lamb.
9:
"And on the sabbath day two lambs of the first year without spot, and two tenth deals of flour for a meat offering, mingled with oil, and the drink offering thereof:" The witnesses note the sabbath offering — two more lambs added, the daily sacrifice doubled. The witnesses note that Gill notes these were just such lambs as appointed for the daily sacrifice. The witnesses note that Henry notes the new moons and sabbaths are often spoken of together as great solemnities, comfortable to the saints and typical of gospel grace; every sabbath the offering was doubled. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the two more lambs — the daily sacrifice doubled; the without spot — the unblemished victims, as the daily; the the holier day — the worship increased with the holiness; the typical of gospel grace — the sabbath a foretaste of the rest in Christ.

The puritan expositor (Henry) marks the fitness of the doubled sabbath offering: as the day was holier, so the sacrifice was increased, the sabbath's worship richer than the common day's; the added lambs teach that the day of rest is to be a day of fuller devotion, not of less, the believer giving more to God on his holy day — and the whole, Henry notes, typical of gospel grace, the sabbath a foretaste of the rest and the richer worship of the people of God in Christ.

So the witnesses make the verse the sabbath offering: The witnesses note the two more lambs: the daily sacrifice doubled. The witnesses note the without spot: the unblemished victims, as the daily. The witnesses note the the holier day: the worship increased with the holiness. The witnesses note the typical of gospel grace: the sabbath a foretaste of the rest in Christ. The expositors observe the two more lambs: the daily sacrifice doubled. The expositors observe the without spot: the unblemished victims, as the daily. The expositors observe the the holier day: the worship increased with the holiness. The expositors observe the typical of gospel grace: the sabbath a foretaste of the rest in Christ. The believer is taught to mark the two more lambs, the without spot, the the holier day, the typical of gospel grace, and to look to the one continual sacrifice. The verse thus records that the sabbath doubled the daily offering — the holier day a day of fuller devotion, typical of gospel grace, the sabbath a foretaste of the rest in Christ.
10:
"This is the burnt offering of every sabbath, beside the continual burnt offering, and his drink offering." The witnesses note the sabbath offering added to, never replacing, the daily continual offering. The witnesses note that Gill notes, with Jarchi, that the burnt offering of one sabbath was not to be offered on another but only on its own; every sacrifice was to be offered in its own season. The witnesses note that the sabbath offering was "beside" the continual one. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the sabbath offering — added to the daily, not instead; the beside the continual — the daily never set aside; the in its own season — each sacrifice kept to its day; the the foundation — the continual offering underlying all.

The witnesses agree the words "beside the continual burnt offering" guard the primacy of the daily sacrifice: the sabbath offering was added to it, never instead of it, the foundation never set aside for the festival; and each sacrifice was to be kept in its own season, the missed offering not made up on another day — teaching the constancy and order of worship, that the special days enrich but never excuse the daily duty, the continual offering underlying all.

So the witnesses make the verse beside the continual: The witnesses note the the sabbath offering: added to the daily, not instead. The witnesses note the beside the continual: the daily never set aside. The witnesses note the in its own season: each sacrifice kept to its day. The witnesses note the the foundation: the continual offering underlying all. The expositors observe the the sabbath offering: added to the daily, not instead. The expositors observe the beside the continual: the daily never set aside. The expositors observe the in its own season: each sacrifice kept to its day. The expositors observe the the foundation: the continual offering underlying all. The believer is taught to mark the the sabbath offering, the beside the continual, the in its own season, the the foundation, and to look to the one continual sacrifice. The verse thus records that the sabbath offering was beside the continual one — the special day enriching but never excusing the daily duty, the continual offering the foundation of all worship.
11:
"And in the beginnings of your months ye shall offer a burnt offering unto the LORD; two young bullocks, and one ram, seven lambs of the first year without spot;" The witnesses note the new-moon offering — two bullocks, a ram, and seven lambs at the beginning of each month. The witnesses note that Gill notes the new moon was religiously observed, marked by the blowing of trumpets over the sacrifices, attendance on the word of the Lord by his prophets, and abstinence from worldly business. The witnesses note that the offering was made on the first day of every month. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the beginning of months — the first day of each month; the two bullocks, a ram, seven lambs — a richer monthly sacrifice; the religiously observed — with trumpets and rest from business; the the months sanctified — every month begun with sacrifice.

The witnesses agree the monthly offering set apart the new moon as a lesser solemnity: a richer sacrifice than the sabbath, with bullocks, ram, and lambs, marked the turning of each month as a fresh dedication of the time to God; the regular return of the new-moon worship taught Israel to begin every month, as every day and every week, with sacrifice — the whole calendar of their life sanctified by the altar, the months as well as the days kept holy to the LORD.

So the witnesses make the verse the new-moon offering: The witnesses note the the beginning of months: the first day of each month. The witnesses note the two bullocks, a ram, seven lambs: a richer monthly sacrifice. The witnesses note the religiously observed: with trumpets and rest from business. The witnesses note the the months sanctified: every month begun with sacrifice. The expositors observe the the beginning of months: the first day of each month. The expositors observe the two bullocks, a ram, seven lambs: a richer monthly sacrifice. The expositors observe the religiously observed: with trumpets and rest from business. The expositors observe the the months sanctified: every month begun with sacrifice. The believer is taught to mark the the beginning of months, the two bullocks, a ram, seven lambs, the religiously observed, the the months sanctified, and to look to the one continual sacrifice. The verse thus records that the new moon was kept with two bullocks, a ram, and seven lambs — each month begun with sacrifice, the whole calendar of Israel's life sanctified by the altar.
12:
"And three tenth deals of fine flour for a meat offering, mingled with oil, for one bullock; and two tenth deals of fine flour for a meat offering, mingled with oil, for one ram;" The witnesses note the meat offerings proportioned to each victim — most for the bullock, less for the ram, less for the lamb. The witnesses note that Gill notes the quantities of flour for each bullock, ram, and lamb are the same as elsewhere, the oil being half a hin for a bullock, a third for a ram, a fourth for a lamb. The witnesses note that the drink offerings of wine were likewise proportioned. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the three tenths for the bullock — the largest meat offering; the two tenths for the ram — a lesser measure; the the oil proportioned — half, a third, a fourth of a hin; the order in worship — each gift measured to its place.

The witnesses agree the graded measures fitted the offering to the victim: the larger the sacrifice, the larger the meat and drink offering that accompanied it, the bullock having most and the lamb least; the careful proportion shows the order of God's worship, nothing left to chance but each gift measured to its place, and teaches that what we bring to God is to be fitting and considered, the accompaniments answering to the offering in due degree.

So the witnesses make the verse the proportioned offerings: The witnesses note the three tenths for the bullock: the largest meat offering. The witnesses note the two tenths for the ram: a lesser measure. The witnesses note the the oil proportioned: half, a third, a fourth of a hin. The witnesses note the order in worship: each gift measured to its place. The expositors observe the three tenths for the bullock: the largest meat offering. The expositors observe the two tenths for the ram: a lesser measure. The expositors observe the the oil proportioned: half, a third, a fourth of a hin. The expositors observe the order in worship: each gift measured to its place. The believer is taught to mark the three tenths for the bullock, the two tenths for the ram, the the oil proportioned, the order in worship, and to look to the one continual sacrifice. The verse thus records that the meat offerings were graded to each victim — the larger sacrifice the larger gift, the order of God's worship, each accompaniment measured to its place.
15:
"And one kid of the goats for a sin offering unto the LORD shall be offered, beside the continual burnt offering, and his drink offering." The witnesses note a kid for a sin offering added at the new moon — even the festival needing atonement. The witnesses note that Gill notes this was a different sort, a sin offering, typical of Christ who was made an offering for sin; it was of the kind that were eaten. The witnesses note that it was offered beside the continual burnt offering. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the a kid for a sin offering — added at the new moon; the beside the continual — joined to the festal worship; the atonement needed — the holy days' services themselves stained; the typical of Christ — who takes away the sin of our holy things.

The witnesses agree the sin offering joined to the festal worship preaches a searching truth: even the holy days, with all their sacrifices, required a sin offering, for the people's very services were stained and needed atonement; the kid of the goats figured Christ, "made sin for us," who takes away the sin of our holy things as well as our other sins — so that the believer's best worship is accepted only through the atoning sacrifice, the sin offering underlying the praise.

So the witnesses make the verse the sin offering: The witnesses note the a kid for a sin offering: added at the new moon. The witnesses note the beside the continual: joined to the festal worship. The witnesses note the atonement needed: the holy days' services themselves stained. The witnesses note the typical of Christ: who takes away the sin of our holy things. The expositors observe the a kid for a sin offering: added at the new moon. The expositors observe the beside the continual: joined to the festal worship. The expositors observe the atonement needed: the holy days' services themselves stained. The expositors observe the typical of Christ: who takes away the sin of our holy things. The believer is taught to mark the a kid for a sin offering, the beside the continual, the atonement needed, the typical of Christ, and to look to the one continual sacrifice. The verse thus records that a kid was offered for a sin offering at the new moon — even the festival needing atonement, figuring Christ who takes away the sin of our holy things, the believer's worship accepted only through atonement.
16:
"And in the fourteenth day of the first month is the passover of the LORD." The witnesses note the Passover on the fourteenth of the first month, the head of the yearly feasts. The witnesses note that Gill notes the month Nisan or Abib, made the first month upon Israel's coming out of Egypt. The witnesses note that Henry notes the appointment here of the Passover sacrifices — not the paschal lamb itself, for which instructions had been given before, but the public offerings of the feast. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the fourteenth — of the first month, Nisan; the the passover of the LORD — the feast of redemption; the the month of the Exodus — commemorating the deliverance; the Christ our passover — the lamb fulfilled in the Lamb slain (1 Cor. 5:7).

The witnesses agree the Passover stands first among the appointed times as the feast of redemption: kept on the fourteenth of the first month, the very month of the Exodus, it commemorated the deliverance from Egypt and headed the sacred year; and the apostle declares its fulfilment — "Christ our passover is sacrificed for us" (1 Cor. 5:7) — the paschal lamb the clearest of all the types of the Lamb slain for his people's redemption.

So the witnesses make the verse the Passover: The witnesses note the the fourteenth: of the first month, Nisan. The witnesses note the the passover of the LORD: the feast of redemption. The witnesses note the the month of the Exodus: commemorating the deliverance. The witnesses note the Christ our passover: the lamb fulfilled in the Lamb slain (1 Cor. 5:7). The expositors observe the the fourteenth: of the first month, Nisan. The expositors observe the the passover of the LORD: the feast of redemption. The expositors observe the the month of the Exodus: commemorating the deliverance. The expositors observe the Christ our passover: the lamb fulfilled in the Lamb slain (1 Cor. 5:7). The believer is taught to mark the the fourteenth, the the passover of the LORD, the the month of the Exodus, the Christ our passover, and to keep the feast in Christ. The verse thus records that the Passover was kept on the fourteenth of the first month — the feast of redemption heading the year, fulfilled in Christ our passover sacrificed for us (1 Cor. 5:7).
17:
"And in the fifteenth day of this month is the feast: seven days shall unleavened bread be eaten." The witnesses note the feast of unleavened bread, beginning the fifteenth, lasting seven days. The witnesses note that Gill notes this is the feast not of the passover (which was the day before) but of unleavened bread, beginning on the fifteenth and lasting seven days. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set the seven days of unleavened bread. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the fifteenth — the feast beginning after the Passover; the seven days — unleavened bread eaten throughout; the no leaven — the purity befitting a redeemed people; the keep the feast — with the unleavened bread of sincerity (1 Cor. 5:8).

The witnesses agree the feast of unleavened bread followed close upon the Passover as its fitting sequel: for seven days no leaven was eaten, in memory of the haste of the Exodus and as a sign of the purity that befits a redeemed people; the apostle draws the gospel meaning — that we keep the feast "not with old leaven... but with the unleavened bread of sincerity and truth" (1 Cor. 5:8), the redeemed called to put away the leaven of sin and live a holy life.

So the witnesses make the verse unleavened bread: The witnesses note the the fifteenth: the feast beginning after the Passover. The witnesses note the seven days: unleavened bread eaten throughout. The witnesses note the no leaven: the purity befitting a redeemed people. The witnesses note the keep the feast: with the unleavened bread of sincerity (1 Cor. 5:8). The expositors observe the the fifteenth: the feast beginning after the Passover. The expositors observe the seven days: unleavened bread eaten throughout. The expositors observe the no leaven: the purity befitting a redeemed people. The expositors observe the keep the feast: with the unleavened bread of sincerity (1 Cor. 5:8). The believer is taught to mark the the fifteenth, the seven days, the no leaven, the keep the feast, and to keep the feast in Christ. The verse thus records that the feast of unleavened bread followed the Passover seven days — the purity befitting the redeemed, fulfilled in keeping the feast with the unleavened bread of sincerity and truth (1 Cor. 5:8).
18:
"In the first day shall be an holy convocation; ye shall do no manner of servile work therein:" The witnesses note the first day of the feast a holy convocation, with rest from servile work. The witnesses note that Gill notes the first of the seven days was kept in a very religious manner. The witnesses note that no servile work was to be done on it. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the first day — the opening of the feast; the an holy convocation — the people assembled for worship; the no servile work — rest from common labour; the a holy rest — a figure of the rest of the redeemed.

The witnesses agree the holy convocation set the day apart for worship and rest: the people were to assemble for sacred service and to cease from their common labour, the day given wholly to God; the rest from servile work marks the sabbath-character of the feast-day, a holy leisure for the worship of God, and a figure of the rest into which the redeemed enter, ceasing from their own works to keep holy day before the LORD.

So the witnesses make the verse the holy convocation: The witnesses note the the first day: the opening of the feast. The witnesses note the an holy convocation: the people assembled for worship. The witnesses note the no servile work: rest from common labour. The witnesses note the a holy rest: a figure of the rest of the redeemed. The expositors observe the the first day: the opening of the feast. The expositors observe the an holy convocation: the people assembled for worship. The expositors observe the no servile work: rest from common labour. The expositors observe the a holy rest: a figure of the rest of the redeemed. The believer is taught to mark the the first day, the an holy convocation, the no servile work, the a holy rest, and to keep the feast in Christ. The verse thus records that the first day was a holy convocation with rest from servile work — the day given wholly to God, a holy rest figuring the rest of the redeemed.
19:
"But ye shall offer a sacrifice made by fire for a burnt offering unto the LORD; two young bullocks, and one ram, and seven lambs of the first year: they shall be unto you without blemish:" The witnesses note the burnt offering of the feast — two bullocks, a ram, and seven lambs, all without blemish. The witnesses note that Gill notes the sacrifice was to be of the creatures next mentioned. The witnesses note that they were to be without blemish, as all the sacrifices. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the two bullocks, a ram, seven lambs — the rich daily burnt offering; the made by fire — a burnt offering unto the LORD; the without blemish — the perfection required of every victim; the the unblemished Lamb — pointing to Christ without spot (1 Pet. 1:19).

The witnesses agree the feast was kept with a rich daily burnt offering, the same as at the new moon: two bullocks, a ram, and seven lambs, all unblemished, offered each of the seven days; the insistence on the victims being "without blemish" runs through the whole calendar, every sacrifice pointing to the one Lamb "without blemish and without spot" (1 Pet. 1:19) — the perfection of the type demanded because the antitype is perfect.

So the witnesses make the verse the feast offering: The witnesses note the two bullocks, a ram, seven lambs: the rich daily burnt offering. The witnesses note the made by fire: a burnt offering unto the LORD. The witnesses note the without blemish: the perfection required of every victim. The witnesses note the the unblemished Lamb: pointing to Christ without spot (1 Pet. 1:19). The expositors observe the two bullocks, a ram, seven lambs: the rich daily burnt offering. The expositors observe the made by fire: a burnt offering unto the LORD. The expositors observe the without blemish: the perfection required of every victim. The expositors observe the the unblemished Lamb: pointing to Christ without spot (1 Pet. 1:19). The believer is taught to mark the two bullocks, a ram, seven lambs, the made by fire, the without blemish, the the unblemished Lamb, and to keep the feast in Christ. The verse thus records that the feast was kept with a rich burnt offering of unblemished victims — the perfection required of every sacrifice pointing to the one Lamb without blemish (1 Pet. 1:19).
20:
"And their meat offering shall be of flour mingled with oil: three tenth deals shall ye offer for a bullock, and two tenth deals for a ram;" The witnesses note the meat offerings of the feast, proportioned to each victim as at the new moon. The witnesses note that Gill notes the quantity of flour for a bullock, a ram, and a lamb is the same as in the foregoing sacrifices. The witnesses note that the meat offering of flour and oil accompanied each burnt offering. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the flour mingled with oil — the meat offering of the feast; the three tenths for the bullock — two for the ram, proportioned; the as the other offerings — the same ordered measures; the field and flock — the fruit of the ground with the blood of the lamb.

The witnesses agree the same ordered measures governed the feast as the other offerings: the flour and oil graded to each victim, three tenths for the bullock, two for the ram, that the gift of the field accompany the gift of the flock in due proportion; the constancy of the rule through every solemnity shows the unchanging order of God's worship, the meat offering ever joined to the burnt offering, the fruit of the ground offered with the blood of the lamb.

So the witnesses make the verse the feast meat offering: The witnesses note the flour mingled with oil: the meat offering of the feast. The witnesses note the three tenths for the bullock: two for the ram, proportioned. The witnesses note the as the other offerings: the same ordered measures. The witnesses note the field and flock: the fruit of the ground with the blood of the lamb. The expositors observe the flour mingled with oil: the meat offering of the feast. The expositors observe the three tenths for the bullock: two for the ram, proportioned. The expositors observe the as the other offerings: the same ordered measures. The expositors observe the field and flock: the fruit of the ground with the blood of the lamb. The believer is taught to mark the flour mingled with oil, the three tenths for the bullock, the as the other offerings, the field and flock, and to keep the feast in Christ. The verse thus records that the meat offerings of the feast were proportioned as before — the fruit of the field joined to the blood of the lamb, the unchanging order of God's worship through every solemnity.
22:
"And one goat for a sin offering, to make an atonement for you." The witnesses note the sin offering of the feast — a goat, to make atonement, even amid the holy convocation. The witnesses note that Gill notes that notwithstanding all their services and sacrifices, and though the day was a holy convocation, there was need of a sin offering to expiate their guilt, typical of Christ who takes away the sins of our holy things. The witnesses note that this sin offering also was of the kind that were eaten. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the one goat — the sin offering of the feast; the to make atonement — even amid the holy convocation; the the services defiled — the holy day needing atonement; the typical of Christ — who takes away the sin of our holy things.

The witnesses agree the sin offering at the feast preaches the same searching truth as at the new moon: the holiest day, with all its worship, still needed atonement, for the people's very services were defiled; the goat figured Christ, "made sin for us," who alone can take away the sin that cleaves even to our holy things — so that the joy of the feast rested, like all acceptable worship, upon the atoning sacrifice, the believer's best service accepted only through the blood that covers its imperfection.

So the witnesses make the verse atonement at the feast: The witnesses note the one goat: the sin offering of the feast. The witnesses note the to make atonement: even amid the holy convocation. The witnesses note the the services defiled: the holy day needing atonement. The witnesses note the typical of Christ: who takes away the sin of our holy things. The expositors observe the one goat: the sin offering of the feast. The expositors observe the to make atonement: even amid the holy convocation. The expositors observe the the services defiled: the holy day needing atonement. The expositors observe the typical of Christ: who takes away the sin of our holy things. The believer is taught to mark the one goat, the to make atonement, the the services defiled, the typical of Christ, and to keep the feast in Christ. The verse thus records that a goat was offered for a sin offering to make atonement, even at the feast — the holiest day needing atonement, figuring Christ who takes away the sin of our holy things.
23:
"Ye shall offer these beside the burnt offering in the morning, which is for a continual burnt offering." The witnesses note all the feast offerings added to, never replacing, the continual daily sacrifice. The witnesses note that Gill notes the feast offerings were beside the daily morning sacrifice, and also the daily evening, though it is not expressed. The witnesses note that Wesley notes the morning sacrifice alone is mentioned because the feast began with it, and chiefly because this alone was doubtful whether it should be offered before the festal sacrifices. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the beside the morning offering — the feast added to the daily lamb; the the evening too — though only the morning is named; the never instead — the foundation not set aside; the the daily underlying all — constant worship the ground of the festal.

The witnesses agree the continual offering held its place beneath all the festal sacrifices: the rich offerings of the feast were added to the daily lamb, never instead of it, the foundation never set aside however great the solemnity; the repeated "beside the continual burnt offering" through the whole chapter presses one lesson — that the daily, ordinary sacrifice underlay all the special ones, as the steady, constant worship of God underlies and supports the great occasions, and the believer's daily looking to Christ is the ground of all his festal joy.

So the witnesses make the verse beside the continual: The witnesses note the beside the morning offering: the feast added to the daily lamb. The witnesses note the the evening too: though only the morning is named. The witnesses note the never instead: the foundation not set aside. The witnesses note the the daily underlying all: constant worship the ground of the festal. The expositors observe the beside the morning offering: the feast added to the daily lamb. The expositors observe the the evening too: though only the morning is named. The expositors observe the never instead: the foundation not set aside. The expositors observe the the daily underlying all: constant worship the ground of the festal. The believer is taught to mark the beside the morning offering, the the evening too, the never instead, the the daily underlying all, and to keep the feast in Christ. The verse thus records that the feast offerings were beside the continual morning sacrifice — the daily lamb underlying all the festal ones, constant worship the ground of every solemnity.
24:
"After this manner ye shall offer daily, throughout the seven days, the meat of the sacrifice made by fire, of a sweet savour unto the LORD: it shall be offered beside the continual burnt offering, and his drink offering." The witnesses note the same rich offering repeated each of the seven days of the feast. The witnesses note that Gill notes the same — two bullocks, a ram, and seven lambs — were offered on every one of the seven days, though only the first and last were holy convocations. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set the daily offering of a sweet savour throughout the seven days. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the after this manner — the same offering each day; the throughout the seven days — the feast filled with sacrifice; the a sweet savour — pleasing to the LORD daily; the constancy of devotion — worship ceasing on none of the days.

The witnesses agree the seven days were kept with unbroken sacrifice: the full burnt offering was repeated each day, a sweet savour to the LORD, the whole week of the feast filled with worship; though only the first and last days were holy convocations, the offering ceased on none, teaching the constancy of devotion through the whole season — the people not content with the opening and closing solemnities but bringing their sacrifice every day, the feast a week of unbroken communion with God.

So the witnesses make the verse the seven days: The witnesses note the after this manner: the same offering each day. The witnesses note the throughout the seven days: the feast filled with sacrifice. The witnesses note the a sweet savour: pleasing to the LORD daily. The witnesses note the constancy of devotion: worship ceasing on none of the days. The expositors observe the after this manner: the same offering each day. The expositors observe the throughout the seven days: the feast filled with sacrifice. The expositors observe the a sweet savour: pleasing to the LORD daily. The expositors observe the constancy of devotion: worship ceasing on none of the days. The believer is taught to mark the after this manner, the throughout the seven days, the a sweet savour, the constancy of devotion, and to keep the feast in Christ. The verse thus records that the rich offering was repeated each of the seven days — the whole feast filled with sacrifice, the constancy of devotion through the whole season, a week of unbroken communion with God.
25:
"And on the seventh day ye shall have an holy convocation; ye shall do no servile work." The witnesses note the seventh day of the feast a holy convocation, like the first, with rest from servile work. The witnesses note that Gill notes the seventh day was a holy convocation as the first. The witnesses note that no servile work was to be done on it. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the seventh day — the close of the feast; the an holy convocation — as the first day; the no servile work — rest from labour again; the the feast framed with rest — a holy beginning and a holy end.

The witnesses agree the feast closed as it opened, with a holy convocation and rest: the seventh day, like the first, was set apart for worship and freed from labour, the week of sacrifice ending in a solemn assembly before God; the matching of the first and last days frames the feast with rest, a holy beginning and a holy end, teaching that the seasons of God's worship are to be entered and left in reverence, the redeemed bounding their festal week with the rest and worship of the LORD.

So the witnesses make the verse the closing convocation: The witnesses note the the seventh day: the close of the feast. The witnesses note the an holy convocation: as the first day. The witnesses note the no servile work: rest from labour again. The witnesses note the the feast framed with rest: a holy beginning and a holy end. The expositors observe the the seventh day: the close of the feast. The expositors observe the an holy convocation: as the first day. The expositors observe the no servile work: rest from labour again. The expositors observe the the feast framed with rest: a holy beginning and a holy end. The believer is taught to mark the the seventh day, the an holy convocation, the no servile work, the the feast framed with rest, and to keep the feast in Christ. The verse thus records that the seventh day closed the feast with a holy convocation and rest, as the first — the feast framed with worship, entered and left in reverence before the LORD.
26:
"Also in the day of the firstfruits, when ye bring a new meat offering unto the LORD, after your weeks be out, ye shall have an holy convocation; ye shall do no servile work:" The witnesses note the day of firstfruits — Pentecost, the feast of weeks — when the new meat offering of the wheat harvest was brought. The witnesses note that Gill notes this was the day of Pentecost, fifty days from the bringing of the sheaf of the wave offering, when the firstfruits of the wheat harvest were brought. The witnesses note that Clarke names it the feast of weeks and of Pentecost (Lev. 23:15). The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the after your weeks — fifty days from the wave sheaf; the a new meat offering — the firstfruits of the wheat harvest; the an holy convocation — a day of thanksgiving and rest; the fulfilled at Pentecost — the Spirit poured out, the firstfruits gathered (Acts 2:1).

The witnesses agree the feast of firstfruits crowned the spring festivals with thanksgiving for the harvest: fifty days after the sheaf was waved, the firstfruits of the wheat were offered, a new meat offering of the new corn, in a holy convocation of joy; the day was richly fulfilled in the gospel, for it was on this very Pentecost that the Spirit was poured out and the firstfruits of the great harvest of souls were gathered into the church (Acts 2:1), the feast of the firstfruits becoming the birthday of the Spirit's ingathering.

So the witnesses make the verse the day of firstfruits: The witnesses note the after your weeks: fifty days from the wave sheaf. The witnesses note the a new meat offering: the firstfruits of the wheat harvest. The witnesses note the an holy convocation: a day of thanksgiving and rest. The witnesses note the fulfilled at Pentecost: the Spirit poured out, the firstfruits gathered (Acts 2:1). The expositors observe the after your weeks: fifty days from the wave sheaf. The expositors observe the a new meat offering: the firstfruits of the wheat harvest. The expositors observe the an holy convocation: a day of thanksgiving and rest. The expositors observe the fulfilled at Pentecost: the Spirit poured out, the firstfruits gathered (Acts 2:1). The believer is taught to mark the after your weeks, the a new meat offering, the an holy convocation, the fulfilled at Pentecost, and to keep the feast in Christ. The verse thus records that the day of firstfruits was kept at Pentecost with the new meat offering of the wheat — fulfilled when the Spirit was poured out and the firstfruits of souls gathered into the church (Acts 2:1).
27:
"But ye shall offer the burnt offering for a sweet savour unto the LORD; two young bullocks, one ram, seven lambs of the first year;" The witnesses note the burnt offering of Pentecost — the same rich sacrifice as the new moon and the feast. The witnesses note that Gill notes the burnt offering was of the same kind and number of creatures as on the first day of the month and the days of unleavened bread, with the same meat offering and a kid for a sin offering. The witnesses note that the offering was a sweet savour unto the LORD. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the two bullocks, a ram, seven lambs — the same rich burnt offering; the a sweet savour — pleasing to the LORD; the with a sin offering — atonement joined to devotion; the one ground of acceptance — the same way of approach at every feast.

The witnesses agree the feast of firstfruits was kept with the same full burnt offering as the other great days: two bullocks, a ram, and seven lambs, with their meat offering and a sin offering, a sweet savour to the LORD; the sameness of the sacrifice through the feasts shows the one ground of all acceptance — the burnt offering of devotion and the sin offering of atonement, repeated at every solemnity, that whether at Passover or Pentecost the people drew near to God in the same appointed way, through sacrifice.

So the witnesses make the verse the Pentecost offering: The witnesses note the two bullocks, a ram, seven lambs: the same rich burnt offering. The witnesses note the a sweet savour: pleasing to the LORD. The witnesses note the with a sin offering: atonement joined to devotion. The witnesses note the one ground of acceptance: the same way of approach at every feast. The expositors observe the two bullocks, a ram, seven lambs: the same rich burnt offering. The expositors observe the a sweet savour: pleasing to the LORD. The expositors observe the with a sin offering: atonement joined to devotion. The expositors observe the one ground of acceptance: the same way of approach at every feast. The believer is taught to mark the two bullocks, a ram, seven lambs, the a sweet savour, the with a sin offering, the one ground of acceptance, and to keep the feast in Christ. The verse thus records that Pentecost was kept with the same full burnt offering and sin offering as the other feasts — one ground of acceptance, the people drawing near at every solemnity through the same appointed sacrifice.
30:
"And one kid of the goats, to make an atonement for you." The witnesses note the sin offering of a kid at Pentecost — atonement again, even on the day of firstfruits. The witnesses note that Gill elsewhere notes the kid of the goats for a sin offering, of the sort that were eaten, typical of Christ made an offering for sin. The witnesses note that even this day of thanksgiving required a sin offering to make atonement. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the one kid — the sin offering of the day; the to make atonement — even on the day of firstfruits; the the gladdest worship — still the worship of sinners; the typical of Christ — his atonement underlying all thanksgiving.

The witnesses agree the sin offering closes the calendar's feasts on the same searching note as it ran through them: even the joyful day of firstfruits, with all its thanksgiving for the harvest, needed a kid for a sin offering to make atonement, for the gladdest worship is still the worship of sinners; the goat figured Christ, made sin for his people, whose atonement underlies all their thanksgiving — so that the harvest joy of Pentecost, like every feast, rested upon the blood that covers the sin even of our praises.

So the witnesses make the verse atonement at Pentecost: The witnesses note the one kid: the sin offering of the day. The witnesses note the to make atonement: even on the day of firstfruits. The witnesses note the the gladdest worship: still the worship of sinners. The witnesses note the typical of Christ: his atonement underlying all thanksgiving. The expositors observe the one kid: the sin offering of the day. The expositors observe the to make atonement: even on the day of firstfruits. The expositors observe the the gladdest worship: still the worship of sinners. The expositors observe the typical of Christ: his atonement underlying all thanksgiving. The believer is taught to mark the one kid, the to make atonement, the the gladdest worship, the typical of Christ, and to keep the feast in Christ. The verse thus records that a kid was offered for atonement even at Pentecost — the gladdest worship still the worship of sinners, the harvest joy resting on the atonement that covers the sin of our praises.
31:
"Ye shall offer them beside the continual burnt offering, and his meat offering, (they shall be unto you without blemish) and their drink offerings." The witnesses note the Pentecost offerings added to the continual daily sacrifice, all the victims without blemish. The witnesses note that Gill notes the daily sacrifice of morning and evening, so often mentioned and inculcated as not to be omitted in any festival, being so necessary and so eminent a type of the great sacrifice of the Messiah. The witnesses note that the victims were to be without blemish, with their drink offerings. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the beside the continual — the feast added to the daily lamb; the without blemish — the perfection of every victim; the with drink offerings — the offerings complete; the the eminent type — the daily lamb pointing to Christ's great sacrifice.

The witnesses agree the chapter closes as it ran, on the primacy of the continual offering and the perfection of the victims: even the feast of firstfruits was kept "beside the continual burnt offering," the daily lamb never set aside, and all the sacrifices "without blemish"; Gill marks the daily offering as the most eminent type of the great sacrifice of the Messiah — so that the whole calendar of worship, from the daily lamb to the yearly feasts, pointed steadily to the one perfect and continual offering of Christ, the foundation and fulfilment of all.

So the witnesses make the verse beside the continual: The witnesses note the beside the continual: the feast added to the daily lamb. The witnesses note the without blemish: the perfection of every victim. The witnesses note the with drink offerings: the offerings complete. The witnesses note the the eminent type: the daily lamb pointing to Christ's great sacrifice. The expositors observe the beside the continual: the feast added to the daily lamb. The expositors observe the without blemish: the perfection of every victim. The expositors observe the with drink offerings: the offerings complete. The expositors observe the the eminent type: the daily lamb pointing to Christ's great sacrifice. The believer is taught to mark the beside the continual, the without blemish, the with drink offerings, the the eminent type, and to keep the feast in Christ. The verse thus records that the Pentecost offerings were beside the continual sacrifice, the victims without blemish — the whole calendar of worship pointing to the one perfect and continual offering of Christ, its foundation and fulfilment.
